preamble: The shift from the high back rounded vowel [u] to the high front unrounded vowel [i] is an extreme transformation involving both Fronting and Unrounding. This often occurs via an intermediate high front rounded [y] stage, where the vowel first fronts and then subsequently loses its rounding.

examples.note: Historical fronting of long back vowels in Scots often resulted in /i/ after unrounding.
Yiddish fronted and unrounded back vowels (via /y/) inherited from Middle High German.
The Greek high back vowel shifted to /y/ and eventually merged with /i/ as part of systemic iotacism.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
Hock, H. H. (1991). Principles of Historical Linguistics.
